position-velocity.storyHook: 🚔 You're a traffic investigator reconstructing a car crash from skid marks and witness accounts. Every number matters — and it all starts with understanding exactly where things were, and how fast they were moving.

storyChunks.title: The Crash Scene
storyChunks.text:
The report says the car started at a position of **−20 m** (20 m west of the reference lamppost) and came to rest at **+40 m** (40 m east of it).

In physics, **position** (x) tells us where an object is relative to a chosen origin. **Displacement** (Δx) is the change in position: Δx = x_final − x_initial.

It's a vector — it has both size and direction. Distance, by contrast, is always positive and tells you the total path length travelled.

question.text: The car started at −20 m and ended at +40 m. What is the displacement?

storyChunks.title: Speed vs Velocity
storyChunks.text:
A witness says the car "was doing about 60 km/h". But was it slowing down, speeding up, or constant?

**Average velocity** = total displacement ÷ total time. It tells you the overall rate of change of position.

**Instantaneous velocity** is the velocity at one specific moment — what the speedometer shows. It equals the slope of the position-time graph at that instant.

**Speed** is the magnitude of velocity — always non-negative.

question.text: A car travels +120 m in 4 s, then −40 m in 2 s. What is the average velocity over the whole trip?
question.feedback: ✅ Total displacement = +120 + (−40) = +80 m. Total time = 6 s. v_avg = 80/6 ≈ 13.3 m/s

storyChunks.title: Acceleration
storyChunks.text:
The skid marks tell you the car decelerated — its velocity changed over time. That rate of change is **acceleration**: a = Δv / Δt.

Acceleration is also a vector. A negative acceleration (deceleration) means the object is slowing down in the positive direction, or speeding up in the negative direction.

On a velocity-time graph, acceleration is the **slope** of the line.

question.text: A car goes from 30 m/s to 6 m/s in 4 s. What is the acceleration?

equations-of-motion.storyHook: 🛸 Mission Control: a probe is descending toward Mars. You have 5 kinematic variables and 5 equations. Choose the right one in 10 seconds or the probe overshoots the landing zone.

storyChunks.title: The Five Variables
storyChunks.text:
Every kinematics problem involving constant acceleration involves just **five variables**:

• **v₀** — initial velocity (m/s)
• **v** — final velocity (m/s)
• **a** — acceleration (m/s²)
• **t** — time (s)
• **s** — displacement (m)

In any given problem, you know three of them. Your job is to find the other two by selecting the equation that links the variables you have.

question.text: You know v₀, a, and t. Which variable can you find directly from v = v₀ + at?
question.feedback: ✅ v = v₀ + at directly gives you final velocity from initial velocity, acceleration, and time.

storyChunks.title: The Five Equations
storyChunks.text:
The five kinematic equations for constant acceleration are:

1. **v = v₀ + at** — missing s
2. **s = v₀t + ½at²** — missing v
3. **v² = v₀² + 2as** — missing t
4. **s = ½(v₀ + v)t** — missing a
5. **s = vt − ½at²** — missing v₀

Selection strategy: identify which variable is unknown and which variable is absent from the problem, then pick the equation that's missing that absent variable.

question.text: You know s, v₀, and v — but not t or a. Which equation lets you find a directly?
question.feedback: ✅ v² = v₀² + 2as contains v, v₀, a, and s — perfect when t is absent.

storyChunks.title: Landing the Probe
storyChunks.text:
The Mars probe enters the atmosphere at **v₀ = 600 m/s** and must decelerate at **a = −50 m/s²** to land safely.

Mission Control needs to know: how far does it travel before stopping?

Knowns: v₀ = 600 m/s, v = 0 m/s, a = −50 m/s²
Missing: t
Use: **v² = v₀² + 2as** → 0 = 600² + 2(−50)s → s = 360 000 / 100 = **3 600 m**

That's exactly 3.6 km — within the safe landing zone. Mission accomplished.

question.text: Using the same probe (v₀=600, a=−50), how long does landing take?

challenge.title: Falling from a Bridge
challenge.description: A stone is dropped from a bridge and hits water 3.2 s later. (a) Find the height of the bridge (ignore air resistance). (b) Find the speed just before impact. (c) A second stone is thrown downward at 5.0 m/s from the same bridge — find its speed on hitting water and its time to fall. (d) Find the vertical separation between the two stones at t = 2.0 s after both are released. Show algebraically that separation = u×t, independent of g. (e) How would air resistance affect the results — which stone is more affected and why?
challenge.hint: Dropped stone: h = ½gt², v = gt. Thrown stone: h = ut + ½gt², v² = u² + 2gh. For (d): both stones share the same g, so their positions differ only by the ut term. Separation = (ut + ½gt²) − ½gt² = ut — it grows linearly and does not depend on g. At t = 2 s: separation = 5 × 2 = 10 m.

free-fall.storyHook: 🪂 You're designing a parachute for a science competition. To get the timing right, you need to understand exactly how gravity — and air resistance — affect a falling object.

storyChunks.title: Galileo vs Aristotle
storyChunks.text:
For 2000 years, people believed Aristotle: heavier objects fall faster. Then **Galileo** tested it. He dropped two balls of different masses from the Tower of Pisa — and they hit the ground at the same time.

In **vacuum** (no air), all objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass, because **gravity accelerates all masses equally**: g ≈ **9.81 m/s²** near Earth's surface.

Air resistance changes everything — which is why a feather falls slowly and a hammer falls fast in air, but land simultaneously on the Moon.

question.text: In a vacuum, which falls faster: a 1 kg ball or a 10 kg ball dropped from the same height?
options: They fall at the same rate
question.feedback: ✅ In vacuum there is no air resistance — both experience the same gravitational acceleration g.

storyChunks.title: The Numbers: h = ½gt²
storyChunks.text:
For a vacuum free fall from rest, the key equations are:

• **v = gt** — velocity increases linearly with time
• **h = ½gt²** — height fallen grows as t squared

With g = 9.81 m/s²:

| Time | Height fallen | Speed |
|------|--------------|-------|
| 1 s  | 4.9 m        | 9.8 m/s |
| 2 s  | 19.6 m       | 19.6 m/s |
| 3 s  | 44.1 m       | 29.4 m/s |
| 4 s  | 78.4 m       | 39.2 m/s |

question.text: A ball is dropped from 45 m in vacuum. Using h = ½gt², approximately how long does it take to land? (g = 10 m/s²)

storyChunks.title: Terminal Velocity
storyChunks.text:
When **air resistance** is present, a falling object doesn't accelerate forever. Drag force increases with speed until it equals gravity — at that point acceleration = 0 and the object reaches **terminal velocity**.

**Terminal velocity** = √(mg / k) where k depends on drag coefficient and cross-sectional area.

A skydiver in free-fall reaches ~55 m/s (200 km/h). With a parachute, the increased drag reduces terminal velocity to ~6 m/s — a safe landing speed.

This is why your parachute design matters: bigger canopy → more drag → slower terminal velocity.

question.text: What happens to the net force on a falling object as its speed increases (with air resistance)?
question.feedback: ✅ As speed increases, drag increases, reducing the net downward force. At terminal velocity, net force = 0.

challenge.title: Figure Skater Spin

challenge.hint: Angular momentum L = Iω is conserved when there is no external torque. Convert rev/s to rad/s: multiply by 2π. Rotational KE = ½Iω². challenge.title: Gyroscopic Bicycle Wheel
challenge.description: A bicycle wheel (treat as ring, mass 1.5 kg, radius 0.35 m) spins at 4 rev/s as the bike moves at 15 km/h. (a) Calculate the wheel's moment of inertia (I = mr² for a ring). (b) Calculate angular momentum L (convert rev/s to rad/s). (c) If the wheel is held horizontal on one end of a 15 cm axle, calculate the precession angular velocity ω_p = mgr/L, where r = 0.15 m. (d) In which direction does the wheel precess? (e) Explain why a faster-spinning wheel precesses more slowly.
challenge.hint: Moment of inertia for a thin ring: I = mR² (R = wheel radius = 0.35 m). Angular velocity: ω = 2π × rev/s. Angular momentum: L = Iω. Precession: the gravitational torque τ = mg × d_arm (where d_arm = 0.15 m is the axle pivot distance). Precession rate: ω_p = τ/L = mg × d_arm / (Iω). Faster spin → larger L → smaller ω_p (slower precession). This gyroscopic effect is what keeps a moving bicycle upright — the spinning wheels resist changes to their orientation.

Why does the Moon have no atmosphere while Earth does?
Escape velocity from Moon ≈ 2.4 km/s; typical thermal speed of molecules at 300 K ≈ 0.5 km/s.
Earth v_esc = 11.2 km/s >> thermal speed, so atmosphere is retained. Moon v_esc is only ~5× thermal speed, and some molecules escape — over time all gas is lost.

challenge.title: Spring Collision on a Track
challenge.description: Trolley A (2 kg) moves at 3.0 m/s east toward stationary trolley B (3 kg) which has a spring (k = 800 N/m) on its front. (a) At maximum spring compression, both trolleys move at the same velocity — find this common velocity using momentum conservation. (b) Find maximum spring compression using energy conservation. (c) Find the final velocities after the spring fully extends (elastic collision — use both conservation laws). (d) Verify that momentum and KE are both conserved in your final answer.
challenge.hint: Common velocity: p_total = (m_A + m_B)v_common. Max compression: ½(m_A+m_B)v_common² + ½kx² = ½m_A u². For elastic collision final velocities: v_A = (m_A−m_B)u/(m_A+m_B), v_B = 2m_A u/(m_A+m_B).

challenge.title: Grandfather Clock Altitude Correction

steps.text: A 0.4 kg mass on a spring (k = 160 N/m) is driven. Find its natural frequency.

Resonance occurs when the driving frequency equals the natural frequency.

Q = f₀ / Δf
A high Q means sharp resonance (low damping); the oscillator rings for a long time.
Explain why the Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapsed in terms of resonance.
Wind-induced vortex shedding occurred at a frequency matching the bridge's natural torsional frequency.
Energy was continuously added (resonance), causing amplitude to grow until structural failure. This is an example of forced resonance.
An undamped oscillator (k = 400 N/m, m = 1 kg) is driven with F₀ = 10 N at resonance. Damping b = 8 N·s/m. Find steady-state amplitude.
At resonance, amplitude A = F₀ / (b·ω₀)

Wave speed v = fλ
An earthquake generates 1 Hz vibrations. A 20-storey building has natural frequency 0.2 Hz. A 5-storey building has 1 Hz. Which is at risk?
The 5-storey building has f₀ = 1 Hz, matching the earthquake frequency → resonance → amplified oscillations → risk of collapse.
The 20-storey building (f₀ = 0.2 Hz ≠ 1 Hz) is not at resonance → less risk.
A bottle with neck area A = 2 cm², neck length l = 3 cm, cavity volume V = 500 cm³ acts as a Helmholtz resonator. Find its frequency.
f = (c/2π)√(A/Vl); using c = 343 m/s and SI units
How does increasing damping affect the resonance peak?
Higher damping b → lower amplitude at resonance, broader peak (lower Q).
The resonance frequency also shifts slightly below ω₀: ω_res = √(ω₀² − b²/2m²).
In MRI, hydrogen nuclei precess at the Larmor frequency. For B = 1.5 T, f₀ = 63.9 MHz. What happens when RF pulses match this frequency?
The RF pulse frequency matches the Larmor frequency → resonance → energy is absorbed by the nuclei.
When RF is removed, nuclei emit energy at f₀ as they relax — detected to form the MRI image.

challenge.title: Earthquake Wave Detection
challenge.description: An earthquake produces P-waves (v = 6000 m/s) and S-waves (v = 3500 m/s). A seismograph records P-waves, then S-waves 28 s later. (a) Set up equations for both arrival times (distance d is unknown) and solve for d. (b) Find the P-wave wavelength if its frequency is 2.0 Hz. (c) Verify the 28 s time difference using your calculated distance. (d) A second station is 840 km from the epicentre — find its P-wave and S-wave arrival time difference. challenge.hint: Both waves travel distance d. Arrival time: t_P = d/v_P, t_S = d/v_S. Time difference: d/v_S − d/v_P = 28 s. Solve for d. S-waves are transverse — they require a medium that can support shear stress (not possible in liquids).

challenge.title: Police Radar Speed Detection
challenge.description: A police radar gun emits 24 GHz microwaves. A car approaches and the reflected signal is 24.00320 GHz. Speed of light c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s. (a) Find the Doppler frequency shift Δf. challenge.title: Testing the Purity of a Crown

challenge.title: Aircraft Wing Lift Calculation
challenge.description: An aircraft wing has total area 80 m². Air flows over the top at 240 m/s and under the bottom at 220 m/s. Air density ρ = 1.25 kg/m³. (a) Use Bernoulli's equation to find the pressure difference between top and bottom surfaces. (b) Calculate the lift force. (c) The aircraft has mass 70,000 kg — does this lift exceed weight? Find the excess lift force. (d) At take-off speed (80 m/s), the speed ratio top:bottom stays the same — recalculate lift. Can it take off? What is the minimum speed needed for lift = weight?
challenge.hint: Bernoulli: P + ½ρv² = constant along a streamline. Faster flow (top surface) means lower pressure, so P_top < P_bottom. Pressure difference: ΔP = ½ρ(v_top² − v_bottom²). Lift force = ΔP × A. At lower aircraft speeds, v_top and v_bottom both scale proportionally, so ΔP ∝ v² — use this to find the minimum take-off speed where Lift = Weight.

challenge.title: Refrigerator Efficiency
challenge.description: A refrigerator removes 150 J of heat from the cold interior per cycle. Its compressor does 60 J of work per cycle. (a) Find the heat dumped to the room (hot reservoir) per cycle. (b) Calculate the actual Coefficient of Performance (COP = Q_cold / W_in). (c) Find the COP of an ideal Carnot refrigerator operating between T_cold = 5°C and T_hot = 25°C (use Kelvin). (d) Compare actual vs Carnot COP. What does the Second Law say about this comparison? (e) Find the minimum work needed per cycle if COP = COP_Carnot.
challenge.hint: Energy conservation: Q_hot = Q_cold + W. COP = Q_cold/W. Carnot COP = T_cold/(T_hot − T_cold) in Kelvin. The 2nd Law states actual COP ≤ Carnot COP — real fridges are always less efficient than ideal.

Explain why electromagnets made of soft iron lose less energy than hard iron for AC applications.
The B–H hysteresis loop area = energy dissipated per cycle. Soft iron has a narrow loop (small area) → less heat loss per cycle.
Hard iron (used for permanent magnets) has a wide loop → large hysteresis losses → unsuitable for AC electromagnets.
An electromagnet and permanent magnet both produce B = 0.5 T. Compare the advantages.
Electromagnet: can be switched on/off, field strength adjustable by varying current, can produce very strong fields with high currents.
Permanent magnet: no electrical power needed, always-on (useful in motors/speakers/sensors), but field cannot be changed. An electromagnet is preferred when controllability is needed.

At saturation: B ≈ μ₀ H_sat (approximately); but B_sat = μ₀μ_r(sat) n I. For a simpler estimate, note μ_r drops near saturation.
Using H = nI and H_sat ≈ B_sat/μ₀ (in air equivalent): I ≈ B_sat/(μ₀ n)

challenge.title: Electromagnetic Crane Design
challenge.description: An electromagnetic crane has a coil of N = 500 turns wound around a steel core (cross-section A = 0.04 m², relative permeability μᵣ = 1000, length L = 0.30 m). Current I = 8 A. μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ T·m/A. (a) Find H inside the core and then B. (b) Calculate magnetic flux Φ = BA. (c) Magnetic pressure on the core surface = B²/(2μ₀). Find the lifting force. (d) To double the lifting force, should you double the current or double the number of turns? Show mathematically why both are equivalent. (e) What is the practical limit on increasing current?
challenge.hint: H = NI/L. B = μ₀μᵣH. Lifting force F = (B²/2μ₀) × A. Since B ∝ NI, Force ∝ (NI)². Doubling N alone multiplies NI by 2, so Force increases by 2² = 4 (quadruples). Doubling I alone has the same effect — also quadruples the force. Both options are equivalent in terms of force increase. To achieve exactly double the force, you would need NI to increase by only √2 ≈ 1.41. The practical limit on increasing current is resistive heating (I²R) in the coil.

Eddy currents are loops of current induced in the bulk core material by changing flux (Faraday's Law). They dissipate power as heat: P = V²/R.

challenge.title: Radio Tuner RLC Circuit
challenge.description: A radio tuner has R = 5 Ω, L = 0.20 mH and a variable capacitor. It must tune to 1.0 MHz (AM radio). (a) Find the required capacitance C for resonance. (b) At resonance, find impedance and rms current if supply is 10 mV rms. (c) Calculate Q-factor = ω₀L/R. (d) Find the bandwidth Δf = f₀/Q. (e) A station broadcasts at 1.01 MHz — is it within the bandwidth? What is the impedance at 1.01 MHz? (f) Explain what a high Q-factor means for the ability to separate stations.
challenge.hint: Resonance: f₀ = 1/(2π√LC). At resonance: Z = R (reactive parts cancel). Q = ω₀L/R. Bandwidth = f₀/Q. High Q → narrow bandwidth → better selectivity → can distinguish nearby stations.

steps.text: A point charge Q = 3 μC. Find the total electric flux through any closed surface surrounding it. (ε₀ = 8.85×10⁻¹² F/m)
Gauss's Law: Φ_E = Q_enc/ε₀

What is the total magnetic flux through any closed surface?
Gauss's Law for magnetism: ∮ B·dA = 0 (no magnetic monopoles exist)
This means magnetic field lines always form closed loops — every north pole is paired with a south pole.
A circular loop (r = 0.1 m) is in a field B increasing at 2 T/s. Find the induced EMF (Faraday's law).
Faraday's Law: ∮ E·dl = −dΦ_B/dt
A capacitor (C = 10 μF) charges with current I = 0.5 A. Find the displacement current between the plates.
Displacement current equals the conduction current in the circuit (Maxwell's addition to Ampere's Law)
What modification did Maxwell add to Ampere's Law and why was it necessary?
Original Ampere: ∮ B·dl = μ₀I_enc. Problem: this failed at a capacitor gap (no conduction current, but B field exists from changing E).
Maxwell added displacement current I_D = ε₀ dΦ_E/dt: ∮ B·dl = μ₀(I + I_D)

Find the intensity of an EM wave with E₀ = 100 V/m. (c = 3×10⁸ m/s, ε₀ = 8.85×10⁻¹² F/m)
Intensity I = ½ε₀cE₀²

challenge.title: Solar Radiation Pressure
challenge.description: The Sun radiates 3.8 × 10²⁶ W total power. Earth orbits at 1.5 × 10¹¹ m. μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s. (a) Find solar intensity (irradiance) at Earth. (b) Find amplitude of the electric field E₀ using I = E₀²/(2μ₀c). (c) Find radiation pressure on a 2 m² perfectly absorbing solar panel (P_rad = I/c). (d) Find the radiation force and compare to the panel's weight (assume 5 kg). (e) Solar sails use radiation pressure — find the sail area needed to produce 1 N of force at Earth's distance.

(c) X-ray: medical imaging, security scanning (λ = 0.01–10 nm). (d) UV: sterilisation, tanning, fluorescence (λ = 10–400 nm).

Wien's Law: λ_max T = b
Arrange in order of penetrating ability (lowest to highest): radio waves, visible light, X-rays, gamma rays.

Order: radio (lowest penetration through matter) < visible < X-rays < gamma rays (highest, can penetrate thick lead).

λ = c/f

challenge.title: Medical Imaging Photon Energies
challenge.description: A chest X-ray uses photons of energy 50 keV. h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, 1 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. (a) Convert X-ray energy to joules, then find frequency and wavelength. (b) Find photon energy (eV) of visible green light (λ = 500 nm). Compare to X-ray — by what factor is an X-ray photon more energetic? (c) UV-B (λ = 310 nm) causes sunburn but visible light (λ = 500 nm) does not despite being brighter — explain using photon energy. challenge.hint: E = hf = hc/λ. The threshold for breaking molecular bonds in DNA is about 3.1 eV (UV range). Photons below this energy can't cause chemical damage regardless of intensity. Microwave energy is too low to break bonds — it heats by making water molecules rotate.

Two plane mirrors face each other at 60°. How many images of an object placed between them are formed?
Number of images n = (360°/θ) − 1
Why are parabolic mirrors used in telescopes and headlights instead of spherical mirrors?
Spherical mirrors suffer from spherical aberration — rays far from the axis focus at slightly different points.
Parabolic mirrors focus all parallel rays to a single exact focal point, eliminating spherical aberration. Essential for high-resolution telescopes and focused headlight beams.

challenge.description: A camera lens has focal length 50 mm. (a) Find image distance when photographing a subject 3.0 m away. How far must the lens move from the film/sensor to focus? Each fringe = half-wavelength path difference (2×mirror movement per fringe)

For destructive interference on reflection: 2nt = λ/2 → t = λ/(4n)

challenge.title: Measuring Laser Wavelength
challenge.description: In a double-slit experiment, slits are 0.40 mm apart, screen is 2.0 m away. With a green laser, 11 bright fringes (5 on each side plus centre) span a total width of 28 mm. (a) Find the fringe spacing. (b) Calculate the laser wavelength. (c) A red laser (λ = 650 nm) replaces the green — find new fringe width for 11 fringes. (d) The slit separation is halved — what happens to fringe spacing? Verify numerically for both lasers. (e) What happens if the slit separation equals the wavelength?

steps.text: A muon lives for t₀ = 2.2 μs in its rest frame. It travels at v = 0.6c. Find its lifetime as measured in the lab.
t = γ t₀

Twin A travels at 0.8c for 6 years (Earth time) and returns. How much does A age?

GPS satellites travel at ~3870 m/s. Find the daily time dilation (SR effect only).
Δt ≈ ½(v/c)² per unit time (for v << c)

Rocket at 0.6c fires a missile at 0.8c relative to itself. Find missile's speed relative to Earth.
Relativistic addition: u' = (u + v)/(1 + uv/c²)
In frame S', two events occur at the same location 3 s apart. In frame S (v = 0.6c), what is the time between events?
This is the proper time interval (same location in S') → dilated in S
An aeroplane carrying an atomic clock flies for 20 h at v = 250 m/s. By how much does it fall behind a ground clock?
Time difference Δt ≈ ½(v/c)² × t
A pion (rest lifetime 26 ns) is accelerated to γ = 30. How far does it travel before decaying?
Lab lifetime: t = γ × t₀ = 30 × 26 ns = 780 ns
Distance (at v ≈ c)
Near a black hole, a clock at r = 1.1 R_s (just outside Schwarzschild radius) ticks at what rate compared to infinity?
Gravitational time dilation: Δτ = Δt √(1 − R_s/r)
The clock near the black hole ticks at only ~30% the rate of a distant clock.

challenge.title: GPS Satellite Clock Correction
challenge.description: GPS satellites orbit at 20,200 km altitude, moving at 3.87 km/s. Special relativity predicts that moving clocks run slow (time dilation). General relativity predicts that clocks at altitude run fast (weaker gravity). This challenge examines the SR effect alone. (a) Find γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²) for the satellite. (b) Using γ ≈ 1 + ½(v/c)², find how many microseconds per day the satellite clock runs slow relative to a ground clock (time lost ≈ ½(v/c)² × 86400 × 10⁶ μs). (c) GPS requires timing accuracy of ~30 ns. Find the GPS position error (in metres) per day if the SR clock drift is uncorrected. (d) The GR effect causes the satellite clock to run fast by +45.9 μs/day. Find the net daily clock error combining SR and GR effects — does the satellite clock run fast or slow overall?
challenge.hint: v/c = 3870 / (3×10⁸) ≈ 1.29 × 10⁻⁵. Binomial approximation: γ ≈ 1 + ½(v/c)². SR time lost per day ≈ ½(v/c)² × 86400 s — convert to μs. Position error = speed of light × time error (in seconds). For (d): net error = GR gain − SR loss = 45.9 − 7.2 = +38.7 μs/day (net gain means satellite clock runs fast — GPS clocks are pre-adjusted to compensate).

Explain why length contraction requires reconsideration of simultaneity.
To measure a moving rod's length, both ends must be recorded simultaneously in the observer's frame.

Proper length L₀ is the length measured in the object's rest frame (longest possible measurement).
Coordinate length L = L₀/γ is measured in a frame where the object is moving. Since γ ≥ 1, L ≤ L₀. Proper length is always the largest.

He-4: mass defect = 0.0304 u. Find binding energy. (1 u = 931.5 MeV/c²)
E_binding = Δm × c² = Δm (in u) × 931.5 MeV/u
Find the kinetic energy of an electron (m = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg) moving at 0.8c.
KE = (γ − 1)mc² where γ = 1/0.6 = 1.667
Find total energy of an electron at 0.8c (m_e = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg).
E_total = γmc² = 1.667 × m_e c²
What is the minimum photon energy needed for electron-positron pair production?
Two particles produced (electron + positron): each has rest energy m_e c²
The Sun converts 4.3×10⁹ kg/s to energy. Find power output.
P = (dm/dt) × c²
In a reaction, reactant masses total 10.0125 u and products total 10.0082 u. Find Q-value in MeV.
Q = Δm × 931.5 MeV/u
An electron and positron annihilate. Find the energy of each photon produced.
Total energy = 2m_e c² = 1.022 MeV, split between 2 photons

workedExamples.title: Speed to Double Rest Mass Energy

steps.text: At what speed does the total relativistic energy equal twice the rest energy?
E_total = γmc² = 2mc² → γ = 2

practiceProblems.template: Calculate the energy equivalent of {m_mg} mg of matter. (c = 3×10⁸ m/s)

steps.text: Write E = mc²
Calculate (ERROR)
State answer

spotTheMistake.explanation: c must be squared: E = 2 × (3×10⁸)² = 2 × 9×10¹⁶ = 1.8×10¹⁷ J.

challenge.title: Nuclear vs Chemical Energy Density
challenge.description: Each U-235 fission releases 200 MeV with a mass defect of ~0.215 u. 1 u = 1.66 × 10⁻²⁷ kg. (a) Convert 200 MeV to joules (1 MeV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ J). (b) Verify using E = mc² with the mass defect. (c) Calculate energy released per kg of U-235 fuel. (d) Burning coal releases ~3 × 10⁷ J/kg. Find how many kg of coal produce the same energy as 1 kg of U-235. (e) A nuclear power station outputs 1 GW. Find daily U-235 consumption in grams.
challenge.hint: Per atom: 200 MeV = 3.2 × 10⁻¹¹ J. Mass of one U-235 atom = 235 × 1.66 × 10⁻²⁷ kg. Energy per kg = (J per atom) / (kg per atom). For (e): mass consumed per second = Power / (energy per kg × efficiency) — assume 33% efficiency.

variables.desc: maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron

steps.text: Light of f = 8×10¹⁴ Hz shines on metal (φ = 2.0 eV). Find KE_max. (h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s)
Einstein's equation: KE_max = hf − φ
A metal has work function φ = 3.0 eV. Find the threshold frequency.
hf₀ = φ → f₀ = φ/h
KE_max of photoelectrons = 1.5 eV. Find the stopping potential.
Stopping potential V_s stops the most energetic electrons: eV_s = KE_max
Light (λ = 250 nm) causes stopping potential of 2.3 V. Find the work function.
Photon energy
The light intensity is doubled but frequency kept constant. How does the photoelectric current change?
Doubling intensity doubles the number of photons per second → double the photoelectrons ejected per second.
Photoelectric current doubles, but KE_max remains unchanged (depends only on frequency, not intensity).
X-ray (λ = 0.05 nm) ejects an electron from a metal (φ = 5 eV). Find KE_max.
Photon energy
KE_max = E − φ ≈ 24.8 keV (much larger than φ)
A graph of KE_max vs frequency gives slope = 6.5×10⁻³⁴ J·s and x-intercept = 5.5×10¹⁴ Hz. Find h and φ.
Slope = h
Work function φ = hf₀ = 6.5×10⁻³⁴ × 5.5×10¹⁴
Find the momentum of a photon of λ = 400 nm visible light.
p = h/λ
A 10 mW laser at λ = 500 nm. Find photons emitted per second.
Energy per photon E = hc/λ = 1240/500 eV = 2.48 eV = 3.97×10⁻¹⁹ J
N/t = P/E_photon
X-ray (λ₀ = 0.1 nm) scatters off an electron at θ = 90°. Find the wavelength shift.
Compton shift: Δλ = (h/m_e c)(1 − cosθ)

practiceProblems.template: Light of frequency {f_PHz} ×10¹⁵ Hz hits a metal with work function φ = {phi_eV} eV. Find max KE of emitted electrons. (h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴, 1 eV = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ J)

spotTheMistake.problem: A student says: 'Brighter light always causes photoelectric emission.'

steps.text: Intensity is proportional to photon number
More photons → more electrons emitted regardless of frequency (ERROR)
Threshold frequency must be met first

spotTheMistake.explanation: Emission only occurs if f > f₀. Below the threshold, no emission occurs regardless of intensity.

challenge.title: Determining Planck's Constant
challenge.description: A student shines UV light of different frequencies onto a sodium surface (work function φ = 2.28 eV) and measures stopping potential V_s. Data: f = 6.0×10¹⁴ Hz → V_s = 0.20 V; f = 7.0×10¹⁴ Hz → V_s = 0.62 V; f = 8.0×10¹⁴ Hz → V_s = 1.04 V. (a) Use any two data points to find Planck's constant h (from eV_s = hf − φ). (b) Find the threshold frequency for sodium. (c) Find maximum speed of electrons at 8.0×10¹⁴ Hz. (d) What is the threshold wavelength? Would red light (λ = 700 nm) eject electrons from sodium?
challenge.hint: From two points: h = eΔV_s/Δf (the gradient of V_s vs f graph, multiplied by e). Threshold: hf₀ = φ, so f₀ = φ/h. KE_max = eV_s = ½m_e v². Check: f of red light vs threshold frequency.

workedExamples.title: de Broglie Wavelength of an Electron

steps.text: An electron (m = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg) moves at 2×10⁶ m/s. Find its de Broglie wavelength.
λ = h/mv

workedExamples.title: de Broglie Wavelength of a Ball

steps.text: A 0.1 kg cricket ball moves at 30 m/s. Find its de Broglie wavelength.
λ = h/(mv)
This wavelength is far smaller than any physical object — wave effects are completely undetectable for macroscopic objects.
Electrons with λ = 0.15 nm are diffracted from crystal planes (d = 0.2 nm). Find the first-order Bragg angle.
Bragg's Law: 2d sinθ = mλ
An electron is confined to a region Δx = 0.1 nm. Find the minimum uncertainty in its momentum.
Heisenberg: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2
An excited state has lifetime Δt = 1 ns. Find the energy uncertainty.
ΔEΔt ≥ ℏ/2
Electrons (54 eV) are reflected from Ni crystal (d = 0.215 nm). Find their de Broglie λ and verify the first diffraction peak.
Electrons are accelerated through V = 100 V. Find their de Broglie wavelength.
KE = eV; p = √(2meV)
In a double-slit experiment, a detector is placed at one slit to determine which slit the electron passes through. What happens to the interference pattern?
Observing which slit is used collapses the wave function — the electron is forced to behave as a particle.
The interference pattern disappears! Wave and particle behaviours are complementary — you cannot observe both simultaneously (Bohr's Complementarity Principle).
An electron is confined in a 1D box of width L = 1 nm. Find its ground state (zero-point) energy.
E₁ = h²/(8mL²)
Compare the de Broglie wavelength of a 100 eV electron to a 100 eV photon.
Photon: λ = hc/E = 1240 eV·nm / 100 eV = 12.4 nm (X-ray range)
Electron: λ = 1.226/√(100) ≈ 0.123 nm (hard X-ray / crystal spacing)
steps.latex: \lambda_{electron} = 0.123\text{ nm}\quad(\sim 100\times\text{ shorter than photon at same energy})

practiceProblems.template: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of a {m_amu} amu particle moving at {v_km}×10³ m/s. (h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴, 1 amu = 1.66×10⁻²⁷ kg)

spotTheMistake.problem: A student calculates λ = mv/h for a particle:

steps.text: Write de Broglie relation
Student's formula (ERROR)
Calculate a large wavelength

spotTheMistake.explanation: The formula is λ = h/(mv). Inverting gives momentum/Planck's constant, which is not wavelength.

challenge.title: Electron Microscope Resolution
challenge.description: An electron microscope accelerates electrons through 100 kV. m_e = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg, e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C, h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J·s. (a) Find the kinetic energy gained (in joules) and the speed of the electrons. (b) Calculate the de Broglie wavelength λ = h/(m_e v). (c) Compare to visible light (λ ≈ 500 nm) — how much better is the theoretical resolution? (d) Give a real-world example of a structure this small that electron microscopes can image. (e) Why can't we use an optical microscope to image a single atom?
challenge.hint: Classical (non-relativistic) approximation: KE = eV = ½mv² → v = √(2eV/m). Note: at 100 kV, v ≈ 0.6c, so this slightly overestimates speed and underestimates wavelength — but is the standard school-level approach. De Broglie wavelength: λ = h/(mv). Compare λ to the 500 nm resolution limit of optical microscopes — the electron wavelength is roughly 10⁵ times shorter, giving electron microscopes atomic-scale resolution (~0.1 nm or better).

steps.text: Find the energy of the n=3 level in hydrogen. (E₁ = −13.6 eV)
E_n = E₁/n²
Hydrogen: n=4 → n=2 transition. Find photon wavelength (Balmer series).
Find the minimum energy needed to ionise a hydrogen atom in its ground state.
Ionisation means removing electron to n = ∞ (E = 0)
Find the radius of the n=2 orbit in hydrogen. (a₀ = 0.0529 nm)
r_n = n² a₀
The n=2→1 Lyman transition emits a photon. Find its wavelength. Compare to the n=3→2 Balmer line.
How many distinct spectral lines can be produced by transitions from n=4 down to n=1 in hydrogen?
Possible transitions: 4→3, 4→2, 4→1, 3→2, 3→1, 2→1
Total = n(n−1)/2 for n levels
An electron bombards a copper atom, knocking out a K-shell electron. The L→K transition emits a Kα X-ray. If ΔE = 8040 eV, find λ.
λ = hc/ΔE = 1240 eV·nm / 8040 eV
Explain why absorption and emission spectra for the same element have lines at the same wavelengths but appear as dark vs bright lines.
Emission: excited atoms drop to lower levels, emitting photons of specific ΔE = hf → bright lines.
Absorption: white light passes through cool gas; photons matching ΔE are absorbed (exciting atoms) → dark lines at same λ. Same transitions, opposite direction.
In the Franck-Hertz experiment with mercury, current drops sharply at V = 4.9 eV. What does this indicate?
Electrons with exactly 4.9 eV transfer all energy to Hg atoms in inelastic collisions, exciting them to the first excited state.
steps.latex: \Delta E_1 = 4.9\text{ eV (first excited state of Hg)}

steps.text: Electrons lose all KE → current drops. This confirmed discrete atomic energy levels experimentally.

workedExamples.title: Energy Level Spacing in a Box

steps.text: An electron in a 0.2 nm box (particle-in-a-box). Find the energy difference E₂ − E₁.
E_n = n²h²/(8mL²)
E₂ = 4E₁ = 37.6 eV; ΔE = E₂ − E₁ = 3E₁ = 28.2 eV

practiceProblems.template: An electron in hydrogen falls from n = {n2} to n = {n1}. Find the energy of the emitted photon (in eV).

spotTheMistake.problem: Student finds E_4 in hydrogen: E_4 = −13.6/4 = −3.4 eV:

steps.text: Write formula
Substitute n=4 (ERROR)
State answer

spotTheMistake.explanation: n must be squared: E_4 = −13.6/16 = −0.85 eV.

challenge.title: Laser Light from Energy Transitions
challenge.description: A helium-neon laser uses neon transitions. The red laser (λ = 632 nm) arises from an energy transition. h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3×10⁸ m/s, 1 eV = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ J. (a) Find the energy of the transition in joules and eV. (b) A HeNe laser outputs 5 mW of power — find the number of photons emitted per second. (c) The laser also produces infrared at λ = 3.39 μm. Find the energy transition in eV. (d) A green HeNe line has transition energy 2.28 eV — find its wavelength. (e) Why do lasers produce a single pure wavelength while hot objects emit a broad spectrum?
challenge.hint: E = hc/λ. Photons per second = Power / E_per_photon. For (e): lasers rely on stimulated emission between two specific energy levels — the emitted photon energy is fixed. Hot objects have atoms with many energy states, producing a continuous range.

steps.text: A radioactive sample has N = 10¹⁵ atoms and λ = 2×10⁻⁵ s⁻¹. Find the activity.
A = λN
A nucleus has λ = 0.05 s⁻¹. Find its half-life.
A sample (t₁/₂ = 8 days) has initial activity 1000 Bq. Find activity after 24 days.
Number of half-lives: 24/8 = 3
N₀ = 10⁶ atoms, λ = 0.1 s⁻¹. Find N after 20 s.
N(t) = N₀ e^{−λt}
Radium-226 undergoes alpha decay. Identify the daughter nucleus.
Alpha particle: ⁴₂He. Subtract from Ra-226 (Z=88, A=226)
Daughter is Radon-222 (Rn, Z=86).
Carbon-14 undergoes beta-minus decay. Write the decay equation.
Beta-minus: neutron → proton, emitting electron and antineutrino
A sample has 25% of the original C-14. t₁/₂(C-14) = 5730 years. Find its age.
25% = (½)²: 2 half-lives have passed
After alpha decay, Thorium-234 is in an excited state. It emits a gamma ray of 0.063 MeV. Find the photon wavelength.
Iodine-131 has t₁/₂ = 8.02 days. Find the decay constant λ.
Complete: ²³⁵U + n → ¹⁴¹Ba + ? + 3n
Conserve mass number: 235+1 = 141 + A + 3×1 → A = 236−144 = 92
Conserve atomic number: 92+0 = 56 + Z + 0 → Z = 36 (Krypton)

practiceProblems.template: A sample starts with {N0} nuclei and has half-life {t_half} hours. How many nuclei remain after {t} hours?

spotTheMistake.problem: N₀=800, t₁/₂=4 min, t=12 min. Find N:

steps.text: Calculate number of half-lives: 12/4 = 3
Calculate N (ERROR)
State answer

challenge.title: Medical Radioactive Tracer
challenge.description: Technetium-99m has half-life T½ = 6.0 hours. A patient receives 800 MBq (megabecquerels). N_A = 6.02×10²³, molar mass ≈ 99 g/mol. (a) Find the decay constant λ in s⁻¹. (b) Find the activity after 24 hours and after 48 hours. (c) Find the initial number of Tc-99m atoms (use A = λN). (d) Calculate the initial mass of Tc-99m — comment on how remarkably small it is. (e) The scan must be done when activity exceeds 100 MBq. What is the latest time after injection to perform the scan?
challenge.hint: λ = ln(2)/T½ (convert T½ to seconds). A(t) = A₀ × (½)^(t/T½). N = A/λ. Mass = N × M/N_A. For (e): 100 = 800 × (½)^(t/6 hours) — solve for t.

steps.text: Fission of U-235 releases 200 MeV. Find the energy from 1 kg of U-235 (A=235, Avogadro = 6.022×10²³).
Number of nuclei in 1 kg
Total energy
D-T fusion: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n. Masses: D=2.01410 u, T=3.01605 u, He=4.00260 u, n=1.00867 u. Find energy released.
Energy Q = 0.01888 × 931.5 MeV/u = 17.6 MeV
He-4 has binding energy 28.3 MeV. Find binding energy per nucleon.
Binding energy per nucleon = B.E. / A
In a fission chain reaction, each fission triggers k new fissions. If k = 1.05, how many fissions after 10 generations starting from 1?
Geometric progression: N = k^n
About 1.6 fissions per original — small k > 1 → supercritical → exponentially growing reaction (basis of nuclear weapon or uncontrolled reactor).
Explain why a critical mass is needed for a sustained chain reaction.
Below critical mass, too many neutrons escape the surface without causing fission → k < 1 → reaction dies.
At critical mass, surface-to-volume ratio is small enough that average neutron produces exactly 1 new fission → k = 1 → self-sustaining controlled reaction (nuclear reactor).
steps.latex: k = 1\text{ (critical)}: \text{each fission} \to 1\text{ new fission on average}

steps.text: Fusion requires nuclei to overcome Coulomb repulsion. Estimate the temperature needed for d-t fusion (r ≈ 10⁻¹⁵ m).
PE at r = 10⁻¹⁵ m: U = ke²/r
Set kT ≈ U: T ≈ U/k_B = 2.3×10⁻¹³/1.38×10⁻²³ ≈ 1.7×10¹⁰ K (actual ignition ~10⁸ K due to quantum tunnelling)
steps.latex: T \approx \frac{U}{k_B} \approx 1.7\times10^{10}\text{ K}\quad\text{(tunnelling lowers this to }\sim10^8\text{ K)}

workedExamples.title: Comparing Fission and Fusion Energy Density

steps.text: U-235 fission: 200 MeV/event. D-T fusion: 17.6 MeV/event. Compare energy per unit mass.
Fission per kg U-235: ≈ 8.2×10¹³ J/kg
Fusion per kg D+T (average A=2.5): N = 1000/(2.5×1.67×10⁻²⁷) = 2.4×10²⁹; E = 2.4×10²⁹ × 17.6×1.6×10⁻¹³ ≈ 6.7×10¹⁴ J/kg — ~8× more energy per kg than fission
Explain why fission produces long-lived radioactive waste but fusion does not.
Fission splits heavy nuclei into medium-mass daughter nuclei (e.g., Cs-137, Sr-90) with half-lives of years to thousands of years.
D-T fusion produces He-4 (stable) and a neutron. Neutrons can activate reactor structures, but no long-lived high-level waste is produced directly. Fusion is fundamentally cleaner.
The Sun fuses about 6×10¹¹ kg of hydrogen per second into helium. Find the power output.
About 0.7% of mass is converted to energy in pp chain
Power = (Δm/s) × c²
Natural uranium is 0.72% U-235. Reactor fuel needs ~3%. By what factor must U-235 be enriched?
Enrichment factor
Weapons-grade requires >90% enrichment — 125× enrichment factor (achieved via gas centrifuges).

practiceProblems.template: A nuclear reaction releases {E_MeV} MeV of energy. Convert to joules. (1 MeV = 1.6×10⁻¹³ J)

spotTheMistake.problem: A student says fusion releases less energy per kg than fission:

steps.text: Energy per reaction: fission ~200 MeV, fusion ~17 MeV
Conclusion (ERROR)
Consider mass of fuel

spotTheMistake.explanation: Per unit mass, fusion releases ~4× more energy than fission because the fuel nuclei are ~60× lighter, yet each reaction releases only ~12× less energy.

challenge.title: D-T Fusion Energy Analysis
challenge.description: Deuterium-Tritium fusion: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n. Masses: ²H = 2.01410 u, ³H = 3.01605 u, ⁴He = 4.00260 u, n = 1.00867 u. 1 u = 931.5 MeV. (a) Calculate the mass defect in u. (b) Find energy released per reaction in MeV and joules. (c) Find energy released per kg of D-T fuel (equal masses of D and T, average molar mass ≈ 2.5 g/mol for each). (d) Compare energy density (J/kg) to U-235 fission (~8.2×10¹³ J/kg) and coal (~3×10⁷ J/kg). (e) A fusion reactor outputs 1 GW — find daily fuel consumption in grams.
challenge.hint: Δm = (m_D + m_T) − (m_He + m_n). E = Δm × 931.5 MeV. Per kg: reactions per kg = N_A/M_molar × 1000 (since 1 kg = 1000 g). For (e): assume 33% efficiency. Mass consumed = Power/(efficiency × energy per kg).

steps.text: What are the quark content and charges of a proton?
Proton = uud (two up quarks, one down quark)
Charges: up quarks = +2/3 each, down quark = −1/3
What are the quark content and charge of a neutron?
Neutron = udd (one up, two down)
List the four fundamental forces and their force-carrying bosons.
Strong nuclear: gluons. Electromagnetic: photons. Weak nuclear: W±, Z⁰ bosons.
Gravity: graviton (hypothetical, not yet discovered). Only first three are in the Standard Model.
steps.latex: F_{strong}: g;\quad F_{EM}: \gamma;\quad F_{weak}: W^{\pm}, Z^0

steps.text: In beta-minus decay: n → p + e⁻ + X. What is X? Verify lepton number conservation.
Lepton number before = 0 (n and p are not leptons). After: e⁻ has L = +1, so X must have L = −1.
X is the electron antineutrino: n → p + e⁻ + ν̄_e
What is the antiparticle of the electron? How do they differ and what happens when they meet?
Antielectron = positron (e⁺). Same mass as electron but opposite charge (+e) and opposite lepton number.
Electron + positron → annihilate → 2 photons (pair annihilation). Energy = 2m_e c² = 1.022 MeV.
What is the role of the Higgs boson in the Standard Model?
The Higgs field permeates all space. Particles that interact with this field acquire mass (rest mass proportional to coupling strength).
The Higgs boson is the quantum excitation of this field, discovered at the LHC in 2012 at ~125 GeV/c². Photons and gluons don't interact with the Higgs field → they are massless.
In the reaction p + p → p + p + p + p̄, verify baryon number conservation.
Baryon number: p = +1, p̄ = −1
Why can quarks never be observed in isolation?
The strong force (mediated by gluons) increases with distance — unlike gravity or electrostatics which decrease.
As quarks are pulled apart, the energy stored in the colour field grows until a quark-antiquark pair is created from the vacuum. Free quarks are never observed — only colour-neutral hadrons.
The Standard Model has three generations. List the fermions of the first generation.
Quarks: up (u), down (d). Leptons: electron (e⁻), electron neutrino (ν_e).
Generations 2 and 3 are heavier copies: (c,s,μ,ν_μ) and (t,b,τ,ν_τ). Ordinary matter is built from Generation 1 only.
Describe the W boson's role in beta-minus decay.
In beta decay, a down quark changes to an up quark by emitting a virtual W⁻ boson.
The W⁻ then decays to an electron and electron antineutrino. This is why the weak force has a short range — W bosons are massive (80 GeV/c²).

practiceProblems.template: A proton has rest energy 938.3 MeV. A neutron has 939.6 MeV. Find the mass difference in MeV.

spotTheMistake.problem: A student says quarks can be isolated as free particles:

steps.text: Quarks exist inside hadrons (protons, neutrons)
Applying enough energy can free a quark (ERROR)
Quark confinement: energy creates new quark pairs
steps.latex: E \rightarrow q\bar{q}\,\text{ pairs, not free quarks}

spotTheMistake.explanation: Quark confinement: adding energy creates new quark-antiquark pairs, not free quarks. Quarks cannot be isolated.

challenge.title: Particle Decay Conservation Laws
challenge.description: Check whether each process is allowed by conservation of charge (Q), baryon number (B), and lepton number (L). Show all quantum numbers before and after: (a) n → p + e⁻ + ν̄_e (beta-minus decay). (b) p → e⁺ + γ (proton decaying to positron and photon). (c) μ⁻ → e⁻ + ν̄_e + ν_μ (muon decay). (d) π⁰ → γ + γ (neutral pion decay). (e) For any forbidden process above, state which conservation law is violated. (f) Which fundamental force mediates beta decay?
challenge.hint: Q: proton=+1, neutron=0, electron=-1, positron=+1, photon=0, neutrino=0. Baryon number: proton=neutron=+1, leptons=photons=0. Lepton numbers (L_e, L_μ) are separate and must each be conserved. Electron L_e=+1, positron L_e=−1, ν_e L_e=+1, ν̄_e L_e=−1.

steps.text: Explain the difference between intrinsic and extrinsic (doped) semiconductors.
Intrinsic: pure semiconductor (e.g., Si). At room temperature, some electrons thermally excited to conduction band, leaving holes. Carrier concentration low.
Extrinsic: doped with donor (n-type, extra electrons) or acceptor (p-type, extra holes). Carrier concentration much higher → greater conductivity.
Silicon has band gap E_g = 1.12 eV. Find the maximum wavelength of light that can excite an electron across the gap.
λ_max = hc/E_g = 1240 eV·nm / 1.12 eV
Silicon is doped with phosphorus (donor density N_D = 10¹⁶ cm⁻³). Find majority carrier concentration (assume complete ionisation).
For n-type: n ≈ N_D (majority electrons = donor density)
Minority carriers (holes): p = n_i²/n. For Si at 300 K, n_i ≈ 1.5×10¹⁰ cm⁻³
A silicon sample (n = 10¹⁶ cm⁻³, μ_e = 1400 cm²/V·s, μ_h ≈ 0). Find resistivity.
σ = q n μ_e (n-type, neglect holes)
ρ = 1/σ = 4.46 Ω·m (much higher than metals ~10⁻⁷ Ω·m)
Explain why semiconductor resistance decreases with temperature (opposite to metals).
In metals: carrier density constant, but scattering increases with T → resistance increases.
In semiconductors: carrier density increases exponentially with T (more thermal excitations across band gap) → conductivity increases despite more scattering.
Describe where the Fermi level lies in an n-type semiconductor compared to intrinsic.
In intrinsic Si: Fermi level near mid-gap.
In n-type: Fermi level shifts toward conduction band (more electrons available). Heavier doping → E_F closer to conduction band edge.
A silicon solar cell (E_g = 1.12 eV) is illuminated. Photons of what minimum energy can generate electron-hole pairs?
Photon energy must exceed the band gap
Corresponding maximum wavelength λ = 1107 nm. Photons with λ < 1107 nm (visible and UV) generate carriers; longer-wavelength IR photons do not.
Explain drift and diffusion currents in a semiconductor.
Drift: carriers move under applied electric field E. J_drift = σE = q(nμ_e + pμ_h)E
Diffusion: carriers move from high to low concentration. J_diff = qD(dn/dx). At equilibrium these balance → no net current.
A GaAs LED has band gap E_g = 1.42 eV. Find the emitted photon wavelength.
Photon energy ≈ E_g for direct band gap semiconductor
A semiconductor in B = 0.3 T has current in +x direction. Measured Hall voltage is positive on the +y face. Identify carrier type.
Positive Hall voltage on +y face means positive carriers accumulate there.

Using F = qv × B: current in +x, B in +z, force on positive carriers is in −y → they accumulate on −y face → voltage positive on −y. Here it's positive on +y → NEGATIVE carriers (electrons) → n-type material.

practiceProblems.template: A sample has electron density n = {n_exp}×10¹⁹ m⁻³ and hole density p = {p_exp}×10¹⁹ m⁻³. For μ_e = 0.14, μ_h = 0.045 m²/(V·s) and q = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C. Find conductivity.

spotTheMistake.problem: A student says: 'Semiconductors conduct better at lower temperatures (like metals).'

steps.text: In metals: more collisions at higher T → lower conductivity
Student applies same rule to semiconductors (ERROR)
In semiconductors: more carriers at higher T → higher conductivity

spotTheMistake.explanation: Semiconductors have the opposite temperature behaviour to metals — higher T creates more charge carriers, increasing conductivity.

challenge.title: Doping a Solar Cell
challenge.description: Intrinsic silicon at 300 K: n_i = 1.5×10¹⁰ /cm³. Mobilities: μ_e = 1350 cm²/V·s, μ_h = 480 cm²/V·s. (a) Dope with boron at N_A = 10¹⁶ /cm³ (p-type). Find hole and electron concentrations (use np = n_i²). (b) Calculate conductivity ratio σ_doped/σ_intrinsic. (c) Now dope with phosphorus at N_D = 10¹⁶ /cm³ (n-type). Find carrier concentrations. (d) For a solar cell, which type of silicon should face the sunlight for better electron collection? Explain using carrier mobility values. (e) What happens if both boron and phosphorus are added equally?
challenge.hint: p-type: p = N_A = 10¹⁶, n = n_i²/p. σ ∝ nμ_e + pμ_h. Ratio = (n_doped×μ_e + p_doped×μ_h)/(n_i×μ_e + n_i×μ_h). Electrons have higher mobility → n-type on top captures more current from photogenerated pairs.

variables.desc: thermal voltage × q ≈ 0.026 V at 300 K

steps.text: Describe the formation of the depletion region in a p-n junction.
Electrons diffuse from n to p; holes diffuse from p to n → recombine near junction → ionised donors/acceptors left behind → electric field (built-in potential V_bi).
This field opposes further diffusion until equilibrium: drift current = diffusion current. V_bi ≈ 0.6–0.7 V for silicon.
A silicon diode (I_0 = 10⁻¹⁰ A) is forward-biased at V = 0.65 V (T = 300 K). Find the current.
Shockley equation: I = I₀(e^{qV/kT} − 1). kT/q = 0.0259 V at 300 K
The same diode (I₀ = 10⁻¹⁰ A) is reverse-biased at V = −5 V. Find the current.
For large reverse bias, e^{qV/kT} ≈ 0
Only the tiny leakage current I₀ = 10⁻¹⁰ A = 0.1 nA flows (reverse saturation current).
A Zener diode has V_Z = 5.1 V and is reverse-biased at 6 V through R = 100 Ω. Find the current.
Once breakdown occurs, diode voltage clamps at 5.1 V
Current through resistor
A half-wave rectifier with peak AC voltage 170 V (V_rms ≈ 120 V). Find average DC output voltage. (V_d = 0.7 V diode drop)
Half-wave rectifier average output (ideal): V_avg = V_peak/π
A green LED (λ = 520 nm) emits photons. Estimate the minimum forward voltage.
Photon energy E = hc/λ = 1240/520 = 2.38 eV
Minimum forward voltage ≈ E_g/e ≈ E_photon/e = 2.38 V (the energy gap drives the photon emission)
Explain qualitatively how reverse bias increases depletion width.
Reverse bias widens the depletion region: the applied voltage adds to V_bi, sweeping more majority carriers away from the junction.
Wider depletion → higher capacitance change with voltage → basis of varactor (variable capacitor) diodes.
A solar cell has I_sc = 5 A (short-circuit current) and V_oc = 0.6 V (open-circuit voltage). Find maximum power output (fill factor = 0.75).
Maximum power P_max = FF × I_sc × V_oc
Compare a Schottky diode to a regular p-n junction diode.
Schottky: metal-semiconductor junction. Forward voltage ~0.2–0.4 V (lower than Si p-n ~0.6–0.7 V) → less power loss. No minority carrier storage → faster switching.
steps.latex: V_{Schottky} \approx 0.2-0.4\text{ V vs }0.6-0.7\text{ V for Si p-n}

steps.text: Used in high-speed circuits, RF, switching power supplies. Disadvantage: higher leakage current.
In a tunnel diode, current decreases as forward voltage increases initially. Explain using quantum mechanics.
At low forward voltage, electrons quantum-tunnel through the thin depletion barrier → high current. As V increases, energy band alignment changes — fewer states to tunnel into.
This produces a negative differential resistance region — useful for oscillators and high-speed switching.

practiceProblems.template: A silicon diode has forward voltage drop of 0.7 V. If the supply is {V} V and series resistor is {R} Ω, find the current.

spotTheMistake.problem: A student connects a diode in reverse bias and expects large current:

steps.text: Apply large reverse voltage
Expect normal current flow (ERROR)
Actually: only tiny reverse leakage current flows until breakdown

spotTheMistake.explanation: In reverse bias, the depletion region widens and only a tiny leakage current (nA) flows. Ohm's law does not apply to diodes.

challenge.title: LED Wavelength from Bandgap
challenge.description: Semiconductor bandgap energies: GaAs = 1.42 eV, InGaN (blue) = 2.9 eV, GaP (green) ≈ 2.26 eV. h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3×10⁸ m/s, 1 eV = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ J. (a) Find the peak emission wavelength for each material — state if visible. (b) A white LED combines a blue chip (450 nm) with a yellow phosphor (570 nm). Find the bandgap energies. (c) A GaAs LED is forward-biased at 1.5 V carrying 30 mA. Find electrical power consumed. If light output is 20 mW, find efficiency. (d) Why can't silicon (indirect bandgap, 1.12 eV) be used for LEDs efficiently?
challenge.hint: λ = hc/E_g (convert eV to J first). 1.42 eV → λ = (6.63×10⁻³⁴ × 3×10⁸)/(1.42 × 1.6×10⁻¹⁹). Efficiency = P_light/P_electrical. Silicon has indirect bandgap → photon emission requires a phonon interaction → very inefficient.

steps.text: A BJT has I_C = 4.8 mA and I_B = 60 μA. Find β (h_FE).
β = I_C / I_B
A BJT (β = 150) has I_B = 20 μA. Find I_C and I_E.
I_C = β × I_B
I_E = I_C + I_B
A BJT switch (β = 100, V_CC = 5 V, R_C = 1 kΩ, V_CE(sat) = 0.2 V). Find the base current needed to saturate it.
At saturation: I_C(sat) = (V_CC − V_CE(sat))/R_C
Minimum base current: I_B ≥ I_C/β = 4.8/100 = 48 μA
An n-channel MOSFET has V_T = 2 V. It is biased at V_GS = 4 V, V_DS = 5 V. Is it in saturation or linear region?
Check saturation condition: V_DS ≥ V_GS − V_T
V_DS = 5 V > 2 V → device is in saturation (active) region → acts as current source.
An n-MOSFET (K = 2 mA/V², V_T = 1.5 V) with V_GS = 4 V in saturation. Find I_D.
I_D = (K/2)(V_GS − V_T)²
A CE amplifier has R_C = 4.7 kΩ, r_e = 25 mV/I_C = 25/2 mA = 12.5 Ω (I_C = 2 mA). Find voltage gain.
A_v = −R_C / r_e
An NPN BJT (β = 100) is configured as an inverter (NOT gate). V_CC = 5 V, R_C = 1 kΩ, R_B = 47 kΩ. Input = 5 V. Find V_out.
I_C = βI_B = 9.15 mA; but I_C(sat) = 5/1000 = 5 mA → saturated. V_out = V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V ≈ logic LOW.
Compare FETs and BJTs in terms of control, input impedance, and application.
BJT: current-controlled (I_B controls I_C), lower input impedance (~kΩ). Good for analog amplifiers.
FET (MOSFET): voltage-controlled (V_GS controls I_D), very high input impedance (~GΩ) since gate is insulated. Dominant in digital ICs (CMOS logic), low power.
Two identical BJTs (β = 100 each) are connected as a Darlington pair. Find the composite β.
Darlington pair: β_total ≈ β₁ × β₂
High current gain makes Darlington pairs ideal for driving large currents from small control signals (e.g., motor drivers, relay switches).
In a CMOS inverter, one n-MOSFET and one p-MOSFET are used. Explain operation.
Input HIGH (V_DD): n-MOSFET turns ON, p-MOSFET turns OFF → output connected to ground → LOW.
Input LOW (0V): n-MOSFET turns OFF, p-MOSFET turns ON → output connected to V_DD → HIGH. CMOS draws near-zero static power.

practiceProblems.template: A BJT has β = {beta} and I_B = {IB_uA} μA. Find the collector current.

spotTheMistake.problem: β = 150, I_C = 3 mA. Student finds I_B = β × I_C:

steps.text: Formula: β = I_C / I_B
Solve (ERROR)
State answer

spotTheMistake.explanation: I_B = I_C/β = 0.003/150 = 20 μA. The base current is much smaller than collector current.

challenge.title: BJT Amplifier Design
challenge.description: An NPN BJT has current gain β = 120. Amplifier: V_CC = 12 V, R_C = 1.5 kΩ, R_E = 500 Ω. (a) Base current I_B = 40 μA — find collector current I_C and emitter current I_E. (b) Find V_CE = V_CC − I_C(R_C + R_E). Is the transistor in the active region (V_CE > 0.2 V)? (c) The input signal varies I_B by ±10 μA. Find the peak-to-peak swing in V_CE (output voltage swing). (d) If ΔV_BE = 0.025 V for ΔI_B = 10 μA, find voltage gain A_v = ΔV_CE/ΔV_BE. (e) Why is the output voltage inverted compared to the input?
challenge.hint: I_C = βI_B. I_E = I_B + I_C (exact). For large β (β = 120 here), I_E ≈ I_C since I_B is only ~0.8% of I_C. The description uses the approximation V_CE = V_CC − I_C(R_C + R_E), which is accurate to well within 1% for β = 120. When I_B increases, I_C increases, the voltage drop across R_C increases, so V_CE decreases — the output is phase-inverted relative to the input. Gain A_v = ΔV_CE / ΔV_BE.

steps.text: An AM carrier (f_c = 1 MHz) is modulated by a 5 kHz audio signal. Find the sideband frequencies.
AM sidebands: f_c ± f_m
An AM signal has carrier amplitude V_c = 10 V and message amplitude V_m = 4 V. Find the modulation index m.
m = V_m/V_c
A voice signal with bandwidth 3.4 kHz is used for AM. Find the total transmission bandwidth.
AM bandwidth = 2 × f_message(max)
An FM transmitter (carrier 100 MHz) has a 75 kHz frequency deviation for a 15 kHz audio signal. Find the modulation index β.
β = Δf/f_m
FM: β = 5, message bandwidth f_m = 15 kHz. Find approximate transmission bandwidth.
Carson's Rule: BW ≈ 2(β + 1) × f_m
Compare AM and FM in terms of noise immunity.
AM: information in amplitude → amplitude noise (electrical interference) directly affects signal → poor SNR.
FM: information in frequency deviation → amplitude noise mostly ignored by FM receiver (limiter stage) → FM has ~20 dB better SNR above threshold. FM is preferred for high-quality audio.
Audio with bandwidth 4 kHz is PCM-encoded. Find the minimum sampling rate (Nyquist theorem).
Nyquist: f_s ≥ 2 × f_max
PCM audio: 8-bit samples at 8 kSa/s. Find the bit rate.
Bit rate = samples/s × bits/sample
16-QAM encodes how many bits per symbol? What is the bandwidth efficiency compared to BPSK?
16-QAM: 16 constellation points → log₂(16) = 4 bits per symbol
BPSK: 1 bit/symbol. 16-QAM is 4× more bandwidth-efficient (uses same bandwidth to carry 4× data).
A receiver has signal power S = 10⁻⁶ W and noise power N = 10⁻⁹ W. Find SNR in dB.
SNR in dB

practiceProblems.template: An AM station has carrier frequency {fc_MHz} MHz and maximum audio frequency {fm_kHz} kHz. Find the bandwidth.

spotTheMistake.problem: Student says FM has narrower bandwidth than AM for the same audio signal:

steps.text: AM bandwidth = 2f_m
FM bandwidth = 2f_m (ERROR)
FM bandwidth = 2(Δf + f_m) (Carson's rule)

spotTheMistake.explanation: FM has much wider bandwidth because it includes the frequency deviation Δf. This wider bandwidth allows better noise immunity.

challenge.title: AM vs FM Bandwidth Comparison
challenge.description: An AM station: carrier 900 kHz, carrier power 10 kW, modulation index m = 0.8, audio signal 5 kHz. An FM station: carrier 100 MHz, max frequency deviation Δf = 75 kHz, audio frequency 15 kHz. (a) For AM: find sideband frequencies, total bandwidth, and total power (P_total = P_c(1 + m²/2)) and power in each sideband. (b) For FM: find modulation index β = Δf/f_audio and bandwidth using Carson's rule: BW = 2(Δf + f_audio). (c) AM bandwidth vs FM bandwidth — which uses more spectrum? (d) Explain why FM has better audio quality and noise rejection than AM.
challenge.hint: AM sidebands: f_c ± f_audio. Total bandwidth = 2f_audio = 10 kHz. FM Carson bandwidth = 2(75+15) = 180 kHz. AM is susceptible to amplitude noise; FM encodes audio in frequency, so amplitude noise (lightning, interference) doesn't affect audio quality.

steps.text: Explain ground wave and sky wave propagation.
Ground wave: radio waves follow Earth's curvature by diffraction. Used by AM broadcasts (LF/MF), reliable up to ~1000 km.
Sky wave: HF (3–30 MHz) reflects off the ionosphere → can reach beyond the horizon, 100s–1000s km. Affected by ionospheric conditions (day/night, solar activity).
The ionosphere has electron density n_e = 3×10¹¹ m⁻³. Find the critical frequency f_c for vertical incidence reflection.
f_c = (1/2π)√(n_e e²/ε₀ m_e) ≈ 9√(n_e) (in practical formula with n_e in m⁻³)
The critical frequency is 5 MHz and the angle of incidence is 70°. Find the MUF.
MUF = f_c / cos(θ_i)
Explain skip distance and why there is a dead zone between ground wave and sky wave coverage.
Ground wave attenuates within ~1000 km. Sky wave returns to Earth beyond the skip distance (~typically 100–3000 km).
In between these zones — the 'dead zone' or 'skip zone' — neither ground wave nor sky wave reaches → no reception.
A VHF antenna is 100 m above ground. Find the line-of-sight range. (R_Earth = 6400 km)
d = √(2Rh) for small h
A signal reduces from 1 W to 1 mW over a distance. Find the attenuation in dB.
Attenuation = 10 log(P_out/P_in)
A geostationary satellite is at 36 000 km altitude. Find the propagation delay for uplink + downlink.
One-way delay = d/c = 36000 km / (3×10⁵ km/s)
Round-trip delay = 2 × 0.12 = 0.24 s (noticeable in voice calls — basis for satellite phone delay)
A LEO satellite moves at 7800 m/s toward a ground station transmitting at 1 GHz. Find the Doppler-shifted frequency.
f' = f × (c + v)/c (approaching)
Shift = 26 000 Hz = 26 kHz (must be tracked by receiver)
An optical fibre has bandwidth 50 GHz and uses 8 wavelength channels (WDM). Find total capacity.
Total capacity = bandwidth per channel × number of channels
Using 100 Gbps per channel: total data rate = 800 Gbps = 0.8 Tbps
A 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi signal travels 20 m. Find the free-space path loss.
FSPL = (4πd/λ)²; λ = c/f = 0.125 m
FSPL in dB = 10 log(4.04×10⁶) = 66.1 dB

practiceProblems.template: A signal with wavelength {lam_cm} cm travels {d} m. Find the free-space path loss factor (4πd/λ)².

spotTheMistake.problem: A student says higher frequency waves always travel farther:

steps.text: Higher f → shorter λ → EM wave property
Shorter λ → lower path loss (ERROR)
Correct: shorter λ → higher path loss

spotTheMistake.explanation: Shorter wavelength (higher frequency) means higher free-space path loss. That's why 5G mmWave has shorter range than 4G.

challenge.title: 5G vs 4G Signal Range
challenge.description: 4G LTE operates at 1.8 GHz; 5G mmWave at 28 GHz. c = 3×10⁸ m/s. (a) Find wavelengths of both signals. (b) A brick wall absorbs 90% of signal power. A 4G signal passes through 3 walls — what fraction of original power remains? (c) Free-space path loss L ∝ (f/c × d)². At 100 m, how many dB worse is 5G compared to 4G? (10 × log₁₀(28/1.8)²). (d) 5G mmWave needs a base station every 100–300 m vs 4G's 1–2 km. Calculate the approximate number of base stations needed to cover 1 km² for each. (e) Explain the trade-off: speed vs coverage area.
challenge.hint: λ = c/f. After n walls: P = P₀ × 0.1^n. Path loss ratio in dB = 20 × log₁₀(f_5G/f_4G). For base station density: with radius r, one station covers π r². Number = 1 km²/(π r²). 5G mmWave has Gbps speeds but short range; 4G has less speed but wide coverage.

steps.text: Convert the 8-bit binary number 11010110 to decimal.
Position values (128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1) × bit value
Convert 173 to binary.
Read remainders from bottom up
A signal has maximum frequency 20 kHz (CD audio). Find the minimum sampling frequency.
Nyquist theorem: f_s ≥ 2 f_max
CDs use 44.1 kSa/s (slightly above Nyquist rate for margin).

workedExamples.title: Data Rate from Bandwidth — Shannon's Theorem

steps.text: A channel has bandwidth 3 kHz and SNR = 30 dB. Find the maximum data rate.
SNR = 10^(30/10) = 1000; Shannon: C = B log₂(1 + SNR)
Data byte: 1011010. Add an even parity bit and verify error detection.
Count 1s: there are 4 ones (even). Even parity bit = 0 → transmitted word: 10110100
If one bit flips (e.g., → 10110110): now 5 ones — parity bit fails check → error detected.
The letter 'A' has ASCII code 65. Convert to 8-bit binary.
Four 64 kbps channels are time-division multiplexed. Find the composite bit rate.
TDM: composite rate = sum of individual rates
Using BPSK (1 bit/symbol), what bandwidth is needed for 1 Mbps?
Bandwidth = bit rate / spectral efficiency
Using 16-QAM (4 bits/symbol): BW = 1 Mbps / 4 = 250 kHz (4× more efficient).
Compare optical fibre and copper cable for data transmission.
Optical fibre: bandwidth ~THz (light), low attenuation (~0.2 dB/km), immune to EMI, lightweight. Disadvantage: costly connectors, fragile.
Copper cable: limited bandwidth (~GHz for Cat-8), higher attenuation (~10 dB/km), susceptible to interference. Cheaper, easier to connect. Fibre dominates long-distance; copper used for final metres.
A channel transmits 10⁶ bits and 50 bit errors are detected. Find the bit error rate (BER).
BER = number of errors / total bits transmitted
This is 5 errors per 100 000 bits. Acceptable BER for voice is ~10⁻³; for data 10⁻⁹ is typical requirement.
steps.latex: BER = 5\times10^{-5}\text{ (needs forward error correction for data)}

practiceProblems.template: A channel has bandwidth {B_kHz} kHz and SNR = {SNR}. Find the Shannon channel capacity in kbit/s.

spotTheMistake.problem: A student samples a 4 kHz audio signal at 6 kHz and claims it's sufficient:

steps.text: Nyquist theorem: f_s ≥ 2f_max
Student's claim (ERROR)
Aliasing occurs: 6 < 8 kHz violates Nyquist

spotTheMistake.explanation: 6 kHz < 8 kHz (the Nyquist rate). This causes aliasing — the reconstructed signal will be distorted.

challenge.title: Audio Bitrate and Storage
challenge.description: CD audio: 44,100 Hz sampling rate, 16-bit depth, stereo (2 channels). (a) Calculate the bit rate. (b) Find storage for a 74-minute CD in megabytes (1 MB = 10⁶ bytes). (c) MP3 at 128 kbps compresses the same audio — find the compression ratio and storage for 74 minutes. (d) A phone call uses 8 kHz sampling, 8-bit, mono. Find bit rate and compare to CD. (e) A 2 Mbps internet connection carries voice calls — how many simultaneous calls can it support? Why is digital voice clearer than analogue over long distances?
challenge.hint: Bit rate = sampling rate × bit depth × channels. Storage = bit rate × duration (seconds) / 8 bits per byte, then convert to MB. Compression ratio = CD bit rate / MP3 bit rate. Digital signals can be regenerated perfectly; analogue noise accumulates over distance.
